title: Beginner’s Guide to Exploratory Data Analysis
author: Cole Sheridan
content_html:
Table Overview of Exploratory Data Analysis

What Is Exploratory Data Analysis?

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is the initial process of examining a dataset to understand the structure, patterns, anomalies, and relationships between variables in the data [1].  Rather than starting with a fixed hypothesis or model, EDA follows an open-ended approach.  Through EDA the data itself is able to suggest what questions are worth pursuing.  A useful analogy to think about EDA is to imagine receiving a sealed box filled with unknown components.  Before attempting to assemble anything, one must open the box, inspect each piece, and understand what can be made with the components. 

What EDA Produces (Practical Outputs)

EDA is not just about “looking around” in the dataset, it produces tangible artifacts that guide everything downstream. By the end of EDA, you should be able to point to specific outputs such as:

  • A data dictionary: what each column represents, its units, valid ranges, and assumptions
  • A data quality report: missingness, duplicates, outliers, and parsing issues
  • A risk register: known issues such as leakage risk, confounds, and target ambiguity
  • A modeling readiness decision: whether the dataset is ready for modeling or needs revision

This “deliverable mindset” helps EDA stay systematic and reproducible rather than purely informal exploration.

Real world datasets are frequently messy, incomplete, and potentially misleading.  EDA provides the opportunity to understand what the data truly contains before committing to a modeling decision.  In machine learning, the quality and structure of the data often have a greater impact on the performance rather than the choice of algorithm [2].  This principle is encapsulated in the adage “Garbage In, Garbage Out.”  Applying models without understanding the dataset may lead to spurious correlations, poor performance, or data leakage.  EDA helps understand the nature of the variables, identify limitations in the data, and determine if meaningful learning of a target is possible [1].  EDA also helps reveal considerations on how to preprocess, transform, and filter the data.  Performing EDA early on helps avoid costly modeling mistakes later in the machine learning pipeline.   

EDA is not driven by a specific model or prediction task.  EDA is intentionally exploratory.  This helps ensure that unexpected patterns are not overlooked and that assumptions imposed later by models are grounded in evidence rather than convenience.  However, EDA must also be approached with scrutiny, as early impressions may be misleading if not examined critically [3]. 

A useful real-world habit is to treat your EDA outputs like a lightweight “dataset documentation” record. One well-known proposal is to attach a structured datasheet to datasets that records motivation, composition, collection process, and recommended uses.

EDA vs Confirmatory Data Analysis (CDA)

EDA and CDA often work together, but they serve different purposes:

  • EDA focuses on hypothesis generation and sanity checking (finding patterns, issues, and candidate directions).
  • CDA focuses on hypothesis testing under scrutiny (formal evaluation, controlled analysis, and validation).

EDA asks: “What is this data really saying, and what problems does it contain?”
CDA asks: “Does this claim still hold after we test it carefully?”

A critical principle to emphasize when performing EDA is reproducibility.  While EDA is often informal and investigative, its purpose is not to create simple one-time insights, but to systematically refine the dataset so that analysis may be reliably revisited in the future.   The decisions made during the exploratory data analysis process will directly shape downstream modeling.  It is therefore imperative that these decisions and changes made are reproducible.  Without proper records of exploratory steps, insights lose their ability to be a dependable foundation for CDA.  Thorough documentation of the EDA process and the decisions made therein ensures that results may be replicated, validated, and extended both by the analyst performing EDA and by others. 

Objectives Of EDA

A Simple Order of Operations for EDA

A practical EDA workflow tends to follow a repeatable sequence:

Shape → Types → Missingness → Duplicates → Target sanity → Leakage scan → Distributions → Relationships → Baseline model (optional)

This ordering matters because early issues (like broken data types, duplicated keys, or leakage) can distort everything you see later.

One of the main objectives of EDA is to understand the basic properties of the data.  Statistical summaries of variables such as their mean, median, variance, standard deviation, and range provide insight into the central tendency and spread, indicating how the data is distributed [2].  However, EDA does not end at numerical summaries.  It is equally important to understand the size and structure of the dataset, the types of data contained in the dataset, and how the variables are distributed across observations. 

A second objective in EDA is identifying relationships among variables.  Correlations, dependencies, and interactions between variables help to guide feature engineering and model design.  These relationships may identify variables that could leak information about the target.  Removing and correcting such features is essential for building models that can successfully generalize from the dataset.  Additionally, EDA plays a critical role in detecting anomalies, outliers, and missing values in the dataset, which may heavily influence model behavior if left unchecked. 

EDA Techniques

EDA relies on both visualization and statistical summarization of the data.  Graphs such as scatter plots, histograms, box plots, and quantile-quantile plots assist in seeing patterns that may not be obvious from statistics alone.  While there is some subjective interpretation in graph analysis, visualizations corroborated by statistical analysis and/or multiple visualizations help show evidence of patterns in the dataset [3]. 

Alongside visualization techniques, statistical analysis provides compact descriptions of the data.  A summary statistic provides a single value that characterizes some aspect of the dataset.  These values can provide a quick overview of the data structure or clustering.  Though quick and efficient, caution should be exercised when analyzing statistics, as they can easily conceal important details in the dataset, especially in the presence of extreme values or non-standard distributions [2].

Although EDA does not conform to a specific model, it may include some simple exploratory modeling.  For instance, fitting a basic linear regressor may raise insights about the structure of the data.  A near-perfect fit may indicate data leakage or overly deterministic relationships between variables.  On the opposite end, a poor fit might indicate a non-linear structure.  Though it is equally likely to be caused by missing variables or noisy measurements [1].  When using models in EDA, it is important to remember that the goal of the model is not to validate pre-existing hypotheses about the dataset, but to inform avenues of further investigation.

Common EDA Traps (That Lead to Bad Models)

EDA can be misleading if early impressions are not examined carefully. Common mistakes include:

  • Treating correlation as explanation: relationships may be driven by confounds, duplicated records, or sampling bias
  • Over-cleaning outliers: removing extreme values can delete the true signal (not just noise)
  • Imputing without checking patterns: missing data may reflect a real mechanism (not randomness)
  • Accidentally creating leakage: features may include information from the future relative to the prediction task
  • Believing a single visualization: plots should be cross-checked with statistics and subgroup comparisons

EDA should reduce uncertainty, not create false confidence.

Subcategories Of EDA

EDA is organized according to how many variables are examined at once, being either univariate, bivariate, or multivariate (these are further split into graphical and non-graphical analysis). 

Univariate analysis focuses on a single variable.  Analysis of the variable helps understand its distribution, cleanliness, and variability.  Tools such as summary statistics, histograms, and box plots help determine if a variable is skewed, contains outliers, and the presence of missing values [1]. 

Bivariate analysis examines how pairs of variables relate.  Scatter plots are commonly used to visualize potential associations in numerical data, while correlation coefficients and covariance quantify how strong variables change together.  When variables are categorical using cross-tabulation and contingency tables reveal how frequently combinations of the variables occur together [1].  These relationships help determine whether variables are related, and whether those relationships may be useful for prediction.

Multivariate analysis examines three or more variables to determine how features interact in higher-dimensional space.  Techniques such as pair plots provide an overview of multiple relationships at once.  Unsupervised learning methods such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA) may also be used to help identify the most informative components in the data.  Depending on the nature of the dataset various methods such as time series analysis or spatial analysis may also be appropriate for exploring the dataset [1]. 

Further Considerations

Handling missing data is an important concern in data analysis.  Decisions on whether to remove or impute (replace with plausible values) missing values may significantly impact downstream model results.  Removing data may result in the removal of meaningful information and relationships.  However, even if missing data is imputed, improper imputation may introduce uncertainty and bias.  The effects of both removing and imputing missing data should be considered during the interpretation of results.

In the case of exceedingly large datasets, EDA may involve determining which features are most informative and which features may be discarded.  Reducing the dimensionality of the feature space may help simplify models and improve performance, though careless feature removal may eliminate information or introduce bias [3].  For this reason, making decisions on dropping features should be guided by exploratory findings rather than an arbitrary set of rules.  Additionally, understanding the size and complexity of the dataset helps determine which machine learning methods are feasible.

Conclusion

Exploratory Data Analysis is a foundational step to machine learning.  By carefully examining the data before implementing machine learning models, EDA reveals structure in the dataset, uncovers potential complications, and helps to ensure subsequent analysis is grounded in reality rather than assumption.  Effective EDA is not only about discovering patterns in the dataset, but doing so in a way that is reproducible.  Although EDA is inherently exploratory, the transformations, filtering decisions, and assumptions made during this phase shape downstream results, and must therefore be thoroughly documented.  Reproducibility allows analysts to revisit, validate, and extend prior work, making exploratory insights a reliable foundation for confirmatory analysis and modeling.   

EDA is driven by careful questioning.  It is important to consider the problem the data is intended to address, what each variable represents, and the limitations or quality issues that may exist.  Exploratory Data Analysis contrasts with Confirmatory Data Analysis.  In confirmatory analysis, hypotheses identified during EDA are formally tested to see if they hold under scrutiny.  The creator of EDA, John Tukey, stated


“Unless the detective finds clues, judge or jury has nothing to consider.  Unless exploratory data analysis uncovers indicators, there is like nothing for confirmatory data analysis to consider [4].”

By the End of EDA, You Should Have:

  • A data dictionary (meaning, units, valid ranges)
  • A quality report (missingness, duplicates, outliers, parsing issues)
  • A leakage/confound list (features to block or constrain)
  • A preprocessing plan (encoding, scaling, imputation strategy)
  • A baseline benchmark (even if intentionally simple)
  • A reproducible notebook/script (same results on re-run)

References

[1] F. Hartwig and B. E. Dearing, Exploratory Data Analysis. Thousand Oaks, CA, USA: SAGE Publications, 2021. [Online]. Available: https://methods.sagepub.com/book/mono/exploratory-data-analysis/toc

[2] T. G. Avval and Co-author(s), “The Often-Overlooked Power of Summary Statistics in Exploratory Data Analysis: Comparison of Pattern Recognition Entropy (PRE) to Other Summary Statistics and Introduction of Divided Spectrum-PRE (DS-PRE),” J. Chem. Inf. Model., vol. 61, no. *, pp. –, 2021, doi: 10.1021/acs.jcim.1c00244. [Online]. Available: https://pubs.acs.org/doi/10.1021/acs.jcim.1c00244

[3] S. Ferketich and J. Verran, “Technical Notes,” Research in Nursing & Health, vol. 9, no. 4, pp. 409–*, 1986, doi: 10.1177/019394598600800409. [Online]. Available: https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/019394598600800409

[4] Tukey, J. W. (1977). Exploratory data analysis. Menlo Park: Addison Wesley.

title: Why use stochastic models?
author: igeorgiev3
content_html:
In this blog post, we will explore the importance of stochastic models in the context of unsupervised learning. We will start with k-means clustering, which deterministically clusters points based on heuristics, and build up to Expectation Maximization (EM), which can use any parametrized probabilistic distribution to cluster data.

k-means clustering

k-means clustering is a method used to partition observations into clusters in which each observation belongs to the cluster with the nearest mean. Given a set of observations , we want to partition into sets . We do this by minimizing the intra-cluster squared Euclidean distance:

where is the mean of points in

To give a minimal example of k-means clustering, we are going to generate data according to , where is the identity matrix. Then we’re going to cluster the data with sklearn.cluster.KMeans.

We obtained an accuracy of 96.8%, which is pretty good! Due to its use of Euclidean distance, K-means actually makes an assumption about the underlying data distribution: that it is spherical. These are types of multivariate distributions that are symmetrically distributed in all directions in space, where the distribution does not depend on the direction but only on the distance from the center. A Gaussian distribution is an example of that, and it is exactly what we used to generate our data!

We can actually easily plot the covariance of these classes:

However, if our data doesn’t follow a spherical distribution, then k-means clustering does less well. If we now generate some new data according to

where .

Reapplying k-means on this new data reveals very different results and an accuracy of 46.5%

Visualizing the reach of each cluster reveals exactly why k-means isn’t performing well:

No wonder k-means does poorly. Can we break the assumptions of spherical distributions and just plug in any probabilistic distribution we want? Of course, we can! as long as it is parameterized (and thus solvable) .

Since we want our distribution to best express the true data, we simply have to maximize the expectation of data given some distribution parameters :

Since we are dealing with probabilities, we can instead optimize the log of the expectation to simplify solving

This technique is called Expectation Maximization (EM) and the optimization process can be solved with any optimization approach. If the problem is simple, we can even find an analytical solution. However, for most practical applications, we use Maximum a posteriori estimation (MAP) or simply gradient-based ascent with learning rate :

EM has two main benefits over k-means clustering:

  • Richer data models that can capture more complex distributions.
  • Soft clustering, which computes the probability of each point belonging to a specific cluster.

If we decide to use a normal distribution: then EM simply becomes:

Let’s see this in action!

Our more expressive models netted us a much higher accuracy of 89.2%! Let’s now circle back. What if we assume the covariance matrix is not learnable? Suddenly, our EM algorithm became even simpler:

This update equation is almost exactly an update equation we can use for k-means clustering (which actually only sums over inter-cluster points). As such, k-means clustering can be seen as a special case of Expectation Maximization (EM).

Note that in practice, k-means doesn’t use gradient descent/ascent but instead estimates the means from the data points directly. You can view these two as just different optimization options to solve the same problem.

If we restrict our previous model to have , then we get:

These probabilistic models are way more than classifiers. Since we are learning a probability distribution, we can now sample from it! . They are effectively generative models that can fit data and generate new artificial data!

Takeaways

  • k-means is a simple algorithm that assumes that all data follows a spherical distribution.
  • We can parametrize each cluster as a probabilistic distribution, which gives us more expressiveness.
  • We can then optimize this with Expectation Maximization (EM).

Probabilistic models with EM allow us to:

  • Use more complex models that can capture non-spherical distributions.
  • Soft classify each data point with individual probabilities of belonging to a class.
  • Generate new artificial data.

Like all things, EM isn’t perfect and has a few limitations:

  • It is prone to getting stuck in local maxima, and the solution heavily depends on the initial values. Can you think of any techniques we studied in this course to alleviate this?
  • It still assumes that each class is uni-modal. This can be addressed by using multiple Gaussians and adding them together to form more complex distributions. This is known as Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMS).
  • Heavy reliance on the assumed underlying model. While EM is more expressive than k-means clustering, it still works on an assumption about an underlying distribution, which might not be correct. Non-parametric approaches such as Kernel Density Estimation and Gaussian Processes are alternatives that tackle this issue.

This blog post also comes with an accompanying notebook where you can recreate and play with our results. Notebook

title: Meta Reinforcement Learning
author: adutta85
content_html:
1. Introduction

What is meta reinforcement learning?

Meta learning, or “learning to learn,” enables machine learning models to quickly adapt to new tasks with minimal training data. Unlike traditional machine learning, where models are limited to a specific task, meta learning allows models to learn from various tasks and generalize their knowledge to solve new problems efficiently.

The key idea is to train a model on diverse tasks, allowing it to learn a general learning strategy that can be applied to new tasks, enabling rapid adaptation with few examples. Meta learning algorithms typically involve a two-level optimization process: an inner loop for task-specific learning and an outer loop for updating the learning strategy based on performance across multiple tasks.

Meta reinforcement learning takes this concept further by enabling agents to learn how to learn in reinforcement learning settings. Instead of learning a specific policy for each task, the agent learns a general strategy to adapt quickly to new tasks by learning an update rule or optimization algorithm.

This approach allows meta reinforcement learning agents to exhibit remarkable adaptability and generalization, tackling new challenges more efficiently with limited experience. Meta reinforcement learning is promising for scenarios where agents must continuously adapt to changing environments or handle a wide range of tasks.

2. Meta learning history and origins

Image source

  1. The Early Concepts Phase (1980s-1990s): In the early days of machine learning, researchers began to recognize the limitations of traditional learning algorithms. These algorithms often required large amounts of training data and struggled to generalize effectively to new tasks. Visionary researchers, such as Jürgen Schmidhuber, introduced groundbreaking concepts like “learning to learn” and “self-referential learning” in the late 1980s. These ideas laid the foundation for the field of meta-learning, sparking a new era of research focused on enabling machines to learn how to learn.
  2. The Learning to Learn Phase (1990s-2000s): As the 20th century drew to a close, the focus of meta-learning research shifted towards developing algorithms that could learn and adapt quickly to new tasks with minimal training data. Schmidhuber’s work in the 1990s continued to inspire and influence the field, while researchers like Sebastian Thrun and Lorien Pratt made significant contributions to the understanding of learning to learn. This phase marked a crucial step forward in the quest to create more efficient and generalizable learning algorithms.
  3. The Optimization-Based Meta-Learning Phase (2000s-2010s): With the dawn of the new millennium, meta-learning research entered a new phase characterized by a focus on optimization. Researchers sought to develop meta-learning algorithms that could optimize the learning process itself, enabling models to learn more efficiently and effectively. Sepp Hochreiter and Jürgen Schmidhuber introduced the concept of “learning to learn by gradient descent by gradient descent” in 2001, paving the way for optimization-based meta-learning techniques. Later, in 2016, Marcin Andrychowicz and his colleagues made notable contributions to this area, further advancing the field.
  4. The Few-Shot Learning Phase (2010s): As the 2010s progressed, a new challenge emerged in the world of machine learning: few-shot learning. The goal was to enable models to learn from very few examples, mimicking the remarkable ability of humans to learn quickly from limited experiences. Adam Santoro and his team introduced the concept of “meta-learning with memory-augmented neural networks” in 2016, while Oriol Vinyals and his colleagues proposed the idea of “matching networks for one-shot learning.” These contributions laid the groundwork for few-shot learning techniques that would revolutionize the field.
  5. The Introduction of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) (Finn et al., 2017): In 2017, a significant milestone in meta-learning was achieved with the introduction of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) by Chelsea Finn and her colleagues. MAML provided a general and flexible meta-learning framework that could be applied to various models and tasks. It quickly gained popularity due to its simplicity and effectiveness, becoming a cornerstone of modern meta-learning research.
  6. The Rise of Meta-Reinforcement Learning (2010s-present): Alongside the advancements in few-shot learning, researchers also turned their attention to the realm of reinforcement learning (RL). The goal was to develop RL agents that could adapt quickly to new environments and tasks. In 2016, Jane Wang and her team introduced the concept of “learning to reinforcement learn,” while Yan Duan and his colleagues proposed the idea of “RL^2: Fast Reinforcement Learning via Slow Reinforcement Learning.” These contributions laid the foundation for meta-reinforcement learning, which has since become a thriving area of research.
  7. Recent Advancements (2018-present): In recent years, meta-learning research has continued to make significant strides, with a focus on improving the efficiency, generalization, and applicability of meta-learning algorithms. Nikhil Mishra and his colleagues introduced the concept of “simple neural attentive meta-learner” (SNAIL) in 2018, while Andrei Rusu and his team proposed the idea of “meta-learning with latent embedding optimization” (LEO) in 2019. These advancements have pushed the boundaries of what is possible with meta-learning, opening up new avenues for research and application.

3. What is meta reinforcement learning?

Meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) is a family of machine learning (ML) methods that learn to reinforcement learn. That is, meta-RL uses sample-inefficient ML to learn sample-efficient RL algorithms, or components thereof. As such, meta-RL is a special case of meta-learning with the property that the learned algorithm is an RL algorithm. While there has been significant progress in deep RL over the last several years, with success stories such as mastering the game of Go, stratospheric balloon navigation, or robot locomotion in challenging terrain. RL remains highly sample inefficient, which limits its real-world applications. Meta-RL can produce (components of) RL algorithms that are much more sample efficient than existing RL methods, or even provide solutions to previously intractable problems. While traditional reinforcement learning focuses on learning a specific policy for a single task, meta reinforcement learning takes it a step further by learning a meta-policy—a higher-level strategy that guides the learning process itself. This meta-policy allows the agent to quickly learn policies for a diverse set of tasks, making it more versatile and adaptable.

The Building Blocks of Meta Reinforcement Learning:

  1. Task Distribution: The Foundation of Versatility
    • A diverse set of tasks or environments used to train the meta-learning agent.
    • Carefully designed to cover a wide range of scenarios, promoting generalization.
    • Can include manually crafted tasks or automatically generated variations.
    • Incorporates tasks of varying complexity to challenge the agent’s adaptability.
  2. Meta Policy: The Architect of Learning
    • A higher-level policy that orchestrates the learning process.
    • Maps the agent’s experience and knowledge to policy or model updates.
    • Defines the adaptation strategy for the agent when faced with new tasks.
    • Encapsulates the agent’s learning algorithm, enabling quick adaptation.

The Dual Optimization Dance:

  1. Inner Loop (Adaptation): Mastering the Task at Hand
    • The agent adapts its policy or model for each encountered task.
    • Guided by the meta-policy, the agent updates its parameters based on task-specific experience.
    • The objective is to find an effective policy for the current task.
  2. Outer Loop (Meta-Optimization): Refining the Learning Strategy
    • The meta-policy itself undergoes optimization based on the agent’s performance across multiple tasks.
    • The goal is to discover a meta-policy that enables swift and effective adaptation to new tasks.
    • The outer loop optimization seeks a meta-policy that generalizes well to the task distribution.

3.2 The Meta-Training Journey:

During the meta-training phase, the agent embarks on a journey through the task distribution. It encounters a series of tasks, adapting its policy using the meta-policy and gathering valuable experience along the way. This experience serves as the fuel for updating the meta-policy, refining the agent’s learning strategy. The result is a meta-policy that has learned to learn, ready to tackle new challenges with ease. In meta reinforcement learning, task distribution refers to the process of generating a distribution of tasks that an agent will encounter during its training. These tasks are often variations or instances of a broader task space. The distribution can vary in complexity, diversity, and other factors depending on the objectives of the meta-learning algorithm.

The key aspects of developing an effective task distribution include:

  1. Task Space Definition: The task space defines the range of tasks that the agent may encounter. For example, if the agent is learning to navigate in a maze, the task space might include mazes of different sizes, shapes, and complexities.
  2. Sampling Tasks: Tasks are sampled from the task distribution according to certain criteria. This sampling process can be deterministic or stochastic, and it’s often influenced by the meta-learning algorithm’s objectives. For instance, tasks may be sampled to cover a diverse range of environments or to focus on challenging scenarios that the agent needs to adapt to.
  3. Training on Sampled Tasks: The agent is trained on the sampled tasks, typically using reinforcement learning algorithms. Each task represents a learning episode, where the agent interacts with the environment, receives feedback (rewards), and updates its policy or value function.
  4. Generalization Across Tasks: The goal of meta reinforcement learning is to enable the agent to generalize its learning across tasks, so that it can quickly adapt to new, unseen tasks at test time. This requires the agent to extract common features or patterns from the training tasks that are applicable to novel tasks.
  5. Evaluation: The ultimate test of a meta-learning agent’s effectiveness lies in its ability to generalize to new, unseen tasks. To assess this generalization capability, a separate set of tasks, known as the meta-test set, is used. These tasks are not part of the training task distribution and serve as a measure of the agent’s adaptability. By evaluating the agent’s performance on the meta-test set, we can gauge how well it has learned to learn and its readiness to tackle novel challenges.

Some of the key considerations when developing a task distribution include:

  • Diversity: One of the key aspects of task distribution is diversity. The tasks included in the distribution should cover a broad spectrum of possibilities, each presenting unique challenges and requiring distinct skills. By exposing the agent to a diverse range of tasks, we encourage it to learn a robust and generalizable learning strategy. This diversity helps prevent overfitting, where the agent becomes too specialized in solving specific task types and fails to adapt to new situations.
  • Design: Crafting an effective task distribution is both an art and a science. It requires careful consideration of the problem domain and the desired learning outcomes. Tasks can be manually designed by experts who have deep knowledge of the field, ensuring that the distribution captures the essential challenges and nuances. Alternatively, tasks can be automatically generated through randomization or by sampling from a parametrized family of tasks. Automatic task generation allows for the creation of vast and diverse task distributions, exposing the agent to an even wider range of experiences.
  • Complexity: Another crucial aspect of task distribution is the complexity of the tasks included. A well-designed distribution should incorporate tasks of varying difficulty levels, ranging from simple and easily solvable to more challenging and complex. This gradation in complexity helps the agent learn to handle different levels of difficulty and adapt its learning strategy accordingly. By starting with simpler tasks and progressively increasing the complexity, the agent can build a strong foundation and gradually develop more advanced skills.

3.3 Meta policy and meta learning:

In the context of meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL), a meta-policy plays a role in the agent’s ability to learn effectively across various tasks. The meta-policy defines how the agent should adapt its behavior or parameters based on the information it gathers from different tasks or environments. It encapsulates the agent’s learning strategy, which can be applied to new, unseen tasks. It acts as a high-level learning strategy, guiding the inner loop during its adaptation process on individual tasks. While a standard policy in reinforcement learning maps states to actions, a meta-policy maps the agent’s experience or knowledge to updates in its policy or model. It is typically represented as an update rule or optimization algorithm. The main functions of a meta policy are as follows:

  1. Adaptation Strategy: One of the key functions of a meta-policy is to specify how the agent should adapt its policy parameters or update its policy architecture when presented with a new task. This adaptation strategy can vary depending on the meta-learning algorithm and the characteristics of the tasks.
  2. Policy Parameterization: The meta-policy often parameterizes the policies used by the agent within individual tasks. These policy parameters might include weights in a neural network policy, hyperparameters in a parametric policy, or other factors that influence the agent’s behavior.
  3. Meta-Learning Objective: The meta-policy is trained to optimize a meta-learning objective, which typically involves maximizing the agent’s performance across a distribution of tasks or minimizing its expected loss when adapting to new tasks. This objective guides the meta-learning process, encouraging the meta-policy to discover effective adaptation strategies.

In meta-reinforcement learning, the magic happens through a two-stage training process – the inner loop and the outer loop. Each loop plays a crucial role in equipping the agent with the ability to learn effectively across related tasks. Let’s delve deeper into their specific functions:

Inner Loop: Fast Adaptation on a Single Task

Imagine the inner loop as a fast learner. Here’s what goes down:

  1. Sample a Task: The agent is presented with a specific task from the pre-defined distribution. This task could involve navigating a maze with a particular layout or collecting objects under certain conditions.
  2. Short-term Learning: The agent interacts with the environment for this specific task. Through this interaction, the agent’s policy parameters (think of these as the agent’s “decision-making muscles”) are fine-tuned using standard reinforcement learning techniques. This fine-tuning allows the agent to quickly adapt its behavior to succeed within the specific context of the current task.
  3. Focus on Immediate Reward: The inner loop prioritizes maximizing the reward signal associated with the current task.

Key Takeaway: The inner loop equips the agent with the ability to learn the nuances of a specific task efficiently.

Outer Loop: Learning How to Learn Across Tasks

Think of the outer loop as the wise mentor. It guides the inner loop by:

  1. Meta-Policy Optimization: The outer loop takes a step back and observes how well the agent (inner loop) performed on various sampled tasks. It then uses this information to update the meta-policy, which essentially dictates how the inner loop learns within each task.
  2. Focus on Long-term Adaptability: Unlike the inner loop’s focus on immediate rewards, the outer loop aims for a broader objective. It optimizes the meta-policy to ensure the agent can effectively adapt its learning process across different tasks within the distribution. This is achieved by considering not just the performance on the current task, but also the agent’s ability to learn future, unseen tasks.

The Synergy Between the Loops The inner and outer loop work together in an iterative fashion. Here’s a simplified view of the cycle:

  1. Inner loop learns a specific task (fast adaptation).
  2. Outer loop observes performance and updates the meta-policy (learning to learn).
  3. Repeat with a new task sampled from the distribution.

Over multiple iterations, the agent gets progressively better at adapting its learning process to new tasks within the distribution. This allows it to excel at previously unseen tasks by leveraging the generalizable knowledge acquired through meta-training.

Image source

During meta-training, the agent is presented with a series of tasks sampled from the task distribution. For each task, the agent adapts its policy using the meta-policy and collects experience by interacting with the environment. The collected experience is then used to update the meta-policy in the outer loop optimization. After meta-training, the learned meta-policy can be applied to new, unseen tasks. Given a new task, the agent uses the meta-policy to adapt its policy or model based on the limited experience it gathers from the new task. This allows the agent to quickly find a good policy for the new task without extensive retraining. The combination of meta-policy and meta-learning in meta reinforcement learning enables agents to learn how to learn in the context of sequential decision-making tasks.

4. Meta-RL algorithms:

4.1 MAML (Model agnostic meta-learning)

Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) is a general meta-learning algorithm that can be applied to a variety of learning problems, including classification, regression, and reinforcement learning. The primary goal of MAML is to train a model that can quickly adapt to new tasks using only a small number of gradient updates and a few samples from each task.

The key insight behind MAML is that it optimizes the model parameters to be easily adaptable to new tasks, rather than trying to learn a single model that works well for all tasks. It achieves this by learning a good initialization of the model parameters, such that the model can rapidly learn new tasks with just a few gradient steps.

The MAML algorithm consists of two main stages: meta-training and adaptation. The meta-training algorithm is divided into two parts:

  • Firstly, for a given set of tasks, we sample multiple trajectories using θ and update the parameter using one (or multiple) gradient step(s) of the policy gradient objective. This is called the inner loop.
  • Second, for the same tasks, we sample multiple trajectories from the updated parameters θ’ and backpropagate to θ the gradient of the policy objective. This is called the outer loop.

the model is exposed to a variety of tasks sampled from a task distribution p(T). For each task T_i, the model parameters θ are adapted using K samples from the task, and the adapted parameters θ’i are obtained by taking one or more gradient steps with respect to the task-specific loss L{T_i}. The meta-objective is to optimize the model parameters θ such that the adapted parameters θ’_i minimize the expected loss across all tasks sampled from p(T).

The MAML algorithm for supervised learning is outlined below:

Require: p(T): distribution over tasks
Require: α, β: step size hyperparameters

Randomly initialize θ
while not done do
    Sample batch of tasks T_i ~ p(T)
    for all T_i do
        Sample K datapoints D = {x^(j), y^(j)} from T_i
        Evaluate ∇_θ L_{T_i}(f_θ) using D and L_{T_i}
        Compute adapted parameters with gradient descent: θ'_i = θ - α ∇_θ L_{T_i}(f_θ)
        Sample datapoints D'_i = {x^(j), y^(j)} from T_i for the meta-update
    end for
    Update θ ← θ - β ∇_θ Σ_{T_i~p(T)} L_{T_i}(f_θ'_i) using each D'_i and L_{T_i}
end while

The key advantages of MAML include its flexibility, simplicity, and ability to learn a good initialization for fast adaptation. Some specific strengths are:

  1. Model-agnostic: MAML can be applied to any model trained with gradient descent, including fully connected, convolutional, and recurrent neural networks.
  2. Applicable to a variety of tasks: The algorithm has been successfully applied to classification, regression, and reinforcement learning problems.
  3. Fast adaptation: By learning a good initialization, MAML enables models to adapt to new tasks with just a few gradient steps and a small amount of data.
  4. No additional learned parameters: MAML does not introduce any additional parameters beyond those of the base model, making it more memory-efficient than some other meta-learning approaches.

However, MAML also has some limitations:

  1. Computational overhead: The meta-optimization process involves computing second-order gradients, which can be computationally expensive. The paper proposes a first-order approximation to alleviate this issue.
  2. Assumes task relevance: MAML assumes that the tasks used for meta-training are related and can benefit from a shared initialization. If the tasks are too dissimilar, the algorithm may not learn an effective initialization.
  3. Limited adaptation steps: The model is typically adapted using only a few gradient steps during meta-training and testing. If more extensive adaptation is required for a new task, MAML may not be the most suitable approach.

In meta reinforcement learning, each task T_i is a Markov Decision Process (MDP) defined by an initial state distribution q_i(x_1), transition distribution q_i(x_{t+1} | x_t, a_t), and reward function R_i. The goal is to learn a policy π_θ that can adapt to new tasks with just a few episodes of experience.

To apply MAML to meta reinforcement learning, the algorithm is modified as follows:

Require: p(T): distribution over tasks
Require: α, β: step size hyperparameters

Randomly initialize θ
while not done do
    Sample batch of tasks T_i ~ p(T)
    for all T_i do
        Sample K trajectories D = {(x_1, a_1, ..., x_H)} using f_θ in T_i
        Evaluate ∇_θ L_{T_i}(f_θ) using D and L_{T_i}
        Compute adapted parameters with gradient descent: θ'_i = θ - α ∇_θ L_{T_i}(f_θ)
        Sample trajectories D'_i = {(x_1, a_1, ..., x_H)} using f_θ'_i in T_i
    end for
    Update θ ← θ - β ∇_θ Σ_{T_i~p(T)} L_{T_i}(f_θ'_i) using each D'_i and L_{T_i}
end while

In this meta RL version of MAML, the inner loop samples trajectories (state-action sequences) from the current policy f_θ for each task T_i, and computes the policy gradient to adapt the parameters to θ’_i. The outer loop then updates the initial parameters θ based on the expected loss across all tasks, using trajectories sampled from the adapted policies f_θ’_i.

A few caveats:

  1. Exploration-exploitation trade-off: Balancing exploration and exploitation during meta-training can be difficult, as the agent needs to explore to gather diverse experiences while also exploiting the current policy to optimize performance.
  2. Task distribution: The performance of MAML in meta RL depends on the distribution of tasks used for meta-training. If the tasks are too dissimilar or the distribution is not representative of the test tasks, the learned initialization may not be effective.

4.2 PEARL: Probabilistic Embeddings for Actor-Critic RL

Many current meta-RL algorithms heavily depend on on-policy experience, limiting their sample efficiency. Additionally, they often lack the ability to reason about task uncertainty during adaptation, which can hinder performance in sparse reward scenarios. Probabilistic Embeddings for Actor-Critic RL (PEARL) algorithm proposed by Rakelly & Zhou et al. (2019). PEARL addresses the aforementioned challenges by learning a probabilistic latent context that encapsulates task information and conditioning an off-policy RL algorithm on this context for action selection. The probabilistic context acts as a belief state for the current task, allowing the RL algorithm to differentiate between tasks and enabling the use of off-policy learning in the meta-learning setting.

How does PEARL work?

PEARL consists of two main components:

  1. A probabilistic encoder that captures task uncertainty in a latent context variable Z. The encoder infers the posterior distribution of Z given the agent’s recent experience (context) in a new task.
  2. An off-policy RL algorithm (e.g. Soft Actor-Critic) that learns a policy and value function conditioned on the latent context Z. By conditioning on Z, the policy can adapt its behavior to the current task.

Inference Architecture

The core of PEARL is its inference network, which captures task-relevant information in a latent probabilistic context variable Z. The policy is conditioned on this context, π(a|s, z), allowing it to adapt its behavior to the current task. The inference network uses data from various training tasks to learn how to infer the value of Z from recent experience in a new task.

For each task, the inference network samples a batch of recently collected transitions and encodes each transition cₙ using a network φ to obtain a Gaussian posterior Ψ_φ(z|cₙ). The posteriors are then multiplied to form the overall posterior over the latent context variables.

Image source

The inference network, q_𝜙(z|c) is optimized using a variational lower bound objective:

Considering R as the objective of a downstream task and 𝒩(0, I) as a unit Gaussian prior, one can readily derive this objective by following the derivation of a 𝛽-variational autoencoder, where R represents the reconstruction loss. Empirical findings by Rakelly & Zhou et al. indicate that training the encoder to reconstruct the state-action value function (Q-function) leads to superior performance compared to optimizing it for actor returns (policy maximization) or for reconstructing states and rewards using a VAE structure.

Why Not Use A Deterministic Context? The probabilistic context is advantageous because it can model the belief state of the task, which is crucial for deep exploration in the downstream off-policy algorithm. Deep exploration is particularly beneficial in sparse reward settings, where a consistent exploration strategy outperforms random exploration. This is evident from the results in Section 5 of Osband et al. 2016:

Combining Off-Policy RL with Meta-Learning

Image source

PEARL enables the use of off-policy RL methods in meta-learning by shifting the task inference burden from the RL algorithm to the inference network. This eliminates the need for fine-tuning the RL method during meta-testing and allows the application of off-policy methods during meta-training. The only modification required is conditioning each network on the latent context z.

The official PEARL implementation uses Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) due to its sample efficiency, stability, and probabilistic interpretation. SAC consists of five networks: two state-value functions (V and V̄), two action-value functions (Q₁ and Q₂), and a policy function π. These functions are optimized using specific loss functions.

Meta training:

During meta-training, the agent is exposed to a variety of training tasks. The encoder learns to infer the posterior over Z given context from each task, while the RL algorithm learns a family of policies and value functions conditioned on Z. Crucially, the encoder is trained with on-policy context data to minimize distribution mismatch during adaptation, but the RL algorithm can still leverage off-policy data for efficiency.

The algorithm is as follows:

A simplified explanation is as follow:

  1. Initialize replay buffers to store past experiences.
  2. While training isn’t done:
    • For each task:
      • Sample data using the prior distribution and add it to the replay buffer and context buffer.
      • Sample data using the posterior distribution and add it to the replay buffer and context buffer.
  3. Train the model using the data in the replay buffer and context buffer.

Meta testing:

At meta-test time, the agent is faced with a new task. It initially samples Z from the prior to explore and collect context in the new environment. The collected experience is then used to update the posterior over Z via the encoder. By sampling new Z’s from this updated posterior and conditioning the policy on them, the agent can adapt its behavior to solve the task. This cycle of exploration (by sampling Z from the prior) and adaptation (by inferring the posterior over Z given context) allows the agent to learn efficiently in new environments. The algorithm is as follows:

4.3 RL^2: Fast Reinforcement Learning via Slow Reinforcement Learning

RL^2 (Fast Reinforcement Learning via Slow Reinforcement Learning) is an approach that aims to learn a fast reinforcement learning algorithm encoded in the weights of a recurrent neural network (RNN). The motivation is that deep RL algorithms typically require a huge number of trials to learn sophisticated behaviors. In contrast, animals and humans can learn new tasks in just a few trials by leveraging prior knowledge. RL^2 seeks to bridge this gap – rather than hand-designing a fast RL algorithm, it learns the algorithm end-to-end using a general-purpose “slow” RL algorithm.

The key idea is to use a recurrent neural network to represent the learning algorithm, and learn its weights using standard RL optimization methods. RL^2 offers the potential to learn a learning algorithm that can quickly solve new MDPs by leveraging similarities to previous MDPs that it was trained on. It is a general approach that can in principle be applied to any underlying RL problem.

How RL^2 Works

In RL^2, the learning algorithm is represented by the weights θ of a RNN. The RNN receives all the information a typical RL algorithm would – observations, actions, rewards, termination flags. It also retains its internal state across episodes in a given MDP. So the RNN’s activations essentially store the state of the learning algorithm on the current MDP.

During meta-training, the RNN is trained on a distribution of MDPs ρ_M. An MDP is sampled from ρ_M, the RNN interacts with it for n episodes (trials) and optimizes the return over all n episodes. Then a new MDP is sampled and the process repeats. The RNN weights θ are optimized to maximize the expected total discounted reward accumulated during a trial, averaged across the MDPs in ρ_M. So the RNN is learning to learn – its weights θ encode a learning algorithm that can adapt to the new MDP within a few episodes.

Image source

This process of interaction between an agent and the environment is illustrated in the above figure. Here, each trial, defined as a series of episodes of interaction with a fixed MDP, happens to consist of two episodes, hence n = 2. For each trial, a separate MDP is drawn from ρM, and for each episode, a fresh s0 is drawn from the initial state distribution specific to the corresponding MDP. Upon receiving an action at produced by the agent, the environment computes reward rt, steps forward, and computes the next state st+1. If the episode has terminated, it sets termination flag dt to 1, which otherwise defaults to 0. Together, the next state st+1, action at, reward rt, and termination flag dt, are concatenated to form the input to the policy1, which, conditioned on the hidden state ht+1, generates the next hidden state ht+2 and action at+1. At the end of an episode, the hidden state of the policy is preserved to the next episode, but not preserved between trials.

Maximizing this objective over a trial is equivalent to minimizing the cumulative pseudo-regret. Regret is the difference between the optimal return that could have been obtained if the MDP was fully known, and the actual return obtained by the learning algorithm. Pseudo-regret is a related concept used when the optimal return is not known or not defined. Minimizing regret or pseudo-regret means learning to act optimally in the MDP as quickly as possible.

The formulation presents the agent with a sequence of trials, where each trial contains a different MDP drawn from some distribution. The MDPs vary across trials, but remain fixed within a trial. To perform well on this objective, the agent must learn to adapt its behavior to each new MDP it encounters at the start of a trial.

This requires the agent to integrate information from past actions, rewards, and termination flags to infer what the current MDP might be, and adapt its strategy accordingly. Since different MDPs within the distribution may require different optimal strategies, the agent must learn to differentiate between them based on its experience within a trial. As it accumulates more experience during a trial, the agent should adapt and refine its strategy to maximize performance on the current MDP.

By setting up the problem in this way – forcing the agent to maximize returns over a multi-episode trial on a varying MDP – the authors have created an end-to-end optimization process that encourages the agent (RNN) to learn a “fast” RL algorithm. The process is end-to-end because the RNN is simply trained via RL on this meta-MDP, without needing to explicitly design a separate learning algorithm. The learned algorithm is “fast” in the sense that it must learn to adapt quickly, within a single trial, to each new MDP it encounters. This is driven by the meta-training objective of maximizing trial returns.

Meta-training and Testing

For optimization, RL^2 uses a policy gradient method – specifically Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO). At test time, the RNN with the optimized weights θ is used as a learning algorithm to quickly solve new MDPs drawn from ρ_M. The hope is that it can learn a new MDP in a small number of episodes by leveraging knowledge from the meta-training. The same θ is used to initialize the RNN for each new test MDP.

Key Advantages

  • RL^2 is quite general and can in theory be applied to any RL problem
  • It has the potential to learn learning algorithms that can adapt rapidly to new environments by leveraging past experience
  • The learned algorithms are tailored to the specific distribution of MDPs seen during meta-training

Limitations and Disadvantages

  • RL^2 is computationally expensive as it requires solving a large number of MDPs during meta-training
  • Performance will depend on the meta-training MDP distribution ρ_M being representative of the test MDPs of interest. It may not generalize well to out-of-distribution MDPs.
  • Using a RNN to represent the learning algorithm can make it difficult to understand what algorithm has been learned
  • Long-term credit assignment and optimization can be challenging as the RNN needs to perform well over a sequence of episodes

5. Future directions and challenges:

While Meta-RL has shown promising results in enabling fast adaptation to new tasks, there are still several challenges and future directions to explore to further advance the field. Let’s dive into each of these points in more detail. One of the key challenges in Meta-RL is improving sample efficiency, which refers to the ability to learn from a limited number of samples or experiences. In many real-world scenarios, collecting large amounts of data for each task can be expensive or time-consuming. Therefore, developing Meta-RL algorithms that can efficiently learn from a small number of samples is crucial.

Improving Sample Efficiency in Meta-RL

Sample efficiency is crucial in Meta-RL, especially when dealing with tasks that have limited data. One direction for improvement is to develop more efficient meta-learning algorithms that can adapt to new tasks with fewer samples. Techniques such as better exploration strategies (Gupta et al., 2018), utilizing experience replay (Rakelly et al., 2019), or leveraging hierarchical reinforcement learning architectures (Frans et al., 2018) could help improve sample efficiency. Additionally, methods that incorporate memory mechanisms to retain information from past experiences across tasks may also enhance sample efficiency in Meta-RL (Duan et al., 2016).

Incorporating Prior Knowledge in Meta-RL:

Incorporating prior knowledge into Meta-RL can help bootstrap learning and accelerate adaptation to new tasks. This could involve leveraging transfer learning techniques to initialize the meta-learner with knowledge learned from related tasks or domains (Humplik et al., 2019). Additionally, integrating structured representations of knowledge or meta-priors, such as task hierarchies or task relationships, could guide the meta-learning process and improve generalization to new tasks (Xu et al., 2018). Bayesian approaches or techniques inspired by cognitive science may also offer ways to effectively incorporate prior knowledge into Meta-RL algorithms (Grant et al., 2018).

Addressing the Meta-Overfitting Problem:

Meta-overfitting occurs when a meta-learner performs well on the meta-training tasks but generalizes poorly to unseen tasks. To address this challenge, techniques such as regularization methods (Yin et al., 2020), meta-data augmentation (Rajendran et al., 2020), or meta-learning with diverse task distributions (Mehta et al., 2020) can be employed to encourage the meta-learner to learn more robust and generalizable representations. Moreover, developing meta-learning algorithms that explicitly model uncertainty and adaptability to task distributions may help mitigate the risk of meta-overfitting (Zhao et al., 2020).

Scaling Meta-RL to More Complex Environments:

Scaling Meta-RL to more complex environments involves handling high-dimensional state and action spaces, long-horizon tasks, and diverse task distributions. One approach is to explore hierarchical meta-learning architectures that can learn hierarchical representations of tasks and policies, enabling more efficient learning and adaptation in complex environments (Sæmundsson et al., 2018). Additionally, techniques such as meta-imitation learning, where meta-learning is combined with imitation learning, could help bootstrap learning in complex environments by leveraging expert demonstrations or pre-trained policies (Finn et al., 2017). Furthermore, advancements in hardware acceleration, distributed computing, and algorithmic innovations are essential for scaling Meta-RL to handle increasingly complex environments (Wang et al., 2016).

References

    On Learning now to Learn: The Meta-Meta-Meta…-Hook.” Masters thesis, Technische Universitat Munchen, Germany, 1987.
  2. Thrun, S., Pratt, L. (1998). Learning to Learn: Introduction and Overview. In: Thrun, S., Pratt, L. (eds) Learning to Learn. Springer, Boston, MA.
  3. Hochreiter, S., Younger, A.S., Conwell, P.R. (2001). Learning to Learn Using Gradient Descent. In: Dorffner, G., Bischof, H., Hornik, K. (eds) Artificial Neural Networks — ICANN 2001. ICANN 2001. Lecture Notes in Computer Science, vol 2130. Springer, Berlin, Heidelberg. 
  4. Marcin Andrychowicz, Misha Denil, Sergio Gomez, Matthew W. Hoffman, David Pfau, Tom Schaul, Brendan Shillingford, Nando de Freitas. Learning to learn by gradient descent by gradient descent.
  5. Adam Santoro, Sergey Bartunov, Matthew Botvinick, Daan Wierstra, and Timothy Lillicrap. 2016. Meta-learning with memory-augmented neural networks. In Proceedings of the 33rd International Conference on International Conference on Machine Learning – Volume 48 (ICML’16). JMLR.org, 1842–1850.
  6. Chelsea Finn, Pieter Abbeel, Sergey Levine. 2017. Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning for Fast Adaptation of Deep Networks.
  7. Jacob Beck, Risto Vuorio, Evan Zheran Liu, Zheng Xiong, Luisa Zintgraf, Chelsea Finn, Shimon Whiteson. 2023. A Survey of Meta-Reinforcement Learning.
  8. Kate Rakelly, Aurick Zhou, Deirdre Quillen, Chelsea Finn, Sergey Levine, 2019. Efficient Off-Policy Meta-Reinforcement Learning via Probabilistic Context Variables.

title: Mastering Markov Decision Processes: A Practical RL Journey with OpenAI Gym
author: Aditya Vikram
content_html:
Introduction

Reinforcement Learning (RL) is a powerful subset of machine learning where agents interact with an environment to hone their decision-making skills. At the core of RL lie Markov Decision Processes (MDPs), providing a mathematical structure to define states, actions, rewards, and the dynamics of how an environment transitions over time. OpenAI Gym has become an indispensable toolkit within the RL community, offering a standardized set of environments and streamlined tools for developing, testing, and comparing different RL algorithms.

In this blog post, we’ll dive into practical implementations of classic RL algorithms using OpenAI Gym. We’ll start with a simple MDP and then move onto setting up custom MDPs and tackling the challenges of discretization for continuous state-spaces. Along the way, we’ll also weave in the historical context that shaped these techniques. This article contains relevant code snippets, but you can also follow along by playing around with the Colab notebook gym_examples.ipynb.

Hello World

To get our hands dirty, let’s pick FrozenLake-v0, a simple MDP from Gym’s library. In this grid-world environment, our agent is located at a start tile and aims to reach a goal tile while avoiding holes. The agent can move up, down, left or right at each step. Let’s solve this MDP using the classical algorithms.

1. Value Iteration

Value iteration has roots in the dynamic programming concepts pioneered by Richard Bellman [1]. At its core, this method updates state values based on the values of future states, governed by the Bellman equation. Starting from an initialization of state values (often zeros), each iteration refines value estimates for states using the maximum expected future reward achievable from neighboring states, taking the discount factor into account. We then extract the optimal policy from the converged value function (checkout these lecture scribes for proof of convergence). For the implementation of VI, we use bettermdptools to create the planner object, and run the VI algorithm with it.

Internally, VI iterates until the max update in the value of all states is less than a threshold. In each iteration, we use the Bellman equation to update the value of each state. After convergence, we construct the optimal policy by choosing the action that maximizes the value of each state. The optimal policy learned from Value Iteration can then be used to run an episode of the Frozen Lake problem by iteratively using the policy to step through the states until we hit a terminal state. If you’re following along in the notebook, verify that the agent successfully reaches the goal state and gets a reward of 1.

2. Policy Iteration

Policy Iteration (PI), like Value Iteration, is rooted in dynamic programming principles [2]. PI tackles the MDP by iteratively refining both the policy and its associated value function. Starting with a random policy, each iteration consists of two phases. In the evaluation phase, the value function is estimated under the current policy. Then, the improvement phase leverages these values to greedily update the policy in each state. The algorithm continues iterating until the policy stabilizes (again, refer to these lecture scribes for proof of convergence). The code below implements this algorithm.

Again, the converged policy learned by Policy Iteration algorithm should be able to find the goal state, if you’re following the Colab notebook.

3. Q-Learning

Q-Learning departs from the previous two methods as a model-free, off-policy reinforcement learning algorithm. This means that Q-Learning doesn’t require explicit knowledge of the environment’s transition dynamics. At its core, Q-Learning directly learns the optimal Q-values (action-values) through experience. This is done by iteratively updating Q-values based on the rewards received and an estimate of future Q-values, guided by the Q-learning update rule [3]. The code to use Q-learning via bettermdptools is shown below (lines 1-4). This implementation of Q-learning supports decay for epsilon and learning rates, check the Q-learning readme for more customization.

In the snippet above, I’ve also written a basic epsilon-greedy Q-Learning algorithm, where the agent randomly explores with probability epsilon, while exploiting the best actions based on the learned Q-table otherwise. This implementation is retained for anyone who wants to compare and analyze the benefits of epsilon-decay, otherwise the implementation from bettermdptools should be preferred. Q-Learning relies on a crucial balance of exploration and exploitation to converge to an optimal policy. For anyone wondering, it was important to randomly break ties among all actions that have the same Q-value to make this work. This is because initially all Q-values are zero, so without the randomization, the agent’s trials all led it to holes which give zero reward in Gym’s version of frozen lake. The agent successfully learns to reach the goal state in around 1000 episodes of training for this toy problem. However, for more complicated MDPs, you might need to:

  • Tune the learning rate and exploration rate, and their decay schedules
  • Reduce the MDP size to ensure that the agent has enough chances to learn from rewards
  • Modify the reward structure by introducing more frequent rewards

Custom MDPs: Extending OpenAI Gym’s Reach

While Gym offers a diverse set of environments, sometimes you’ll want to design an MDP specifically tailored to your research or real-world problem. To that end, there is flexibility to create custom MDPs which can also reuse existing Gym components (like different types of spaces – boxes, graphs, etc.). This can be done by extending the gym.Env class, let us go through a simple custom MDP.

Example: The Resource Gathering Problem

Let’s imagine a scenario where an agent navigates a grid world containing resources and obstacles. The goal is to collect as many resources as possible within a limited number of steps. Here’s how we could design this as an MDP:

  • States: The agent’s position on the grid and the number of steps taken.
  • Actions: Movement (up, down, left, right).
  • Transitions: The agent’s movement updates its position on the grid. An episode terminates when the agent hits an obstacle or a resource. We stop after hitting a resource to simplify the custom MDP implementation, otherwise the state space (env.observation_space) becomes a more complicated tuple, and existing PI and VI implementations need to be updated to account for multidimensional state spaces because state would be a tuple of the form (grid_position, resources_collected, steps_taken).
  • Rewards:
    • Positive reward for hitting a resource cell. To make the problem slightly interesting, we uniformly sample the reward from the range [5, 15], to possibly observe the dynamic between higher reward vs longer distance.
    • -100 for hitting an obstacle.
    • -1 per step to encourage efficient solutions.

Implementation

The following gist highlights some of the important aspects of implementing a custom MDP in Gym. It is necessary to define action_space and observation_space attributes of the environment (as per documentation). This environment also supports custom maps taken in the constructor input, otherwise we randomly generate a valid grid. Besides that, we also define the transition matrix self.P for Value Iteration and Policy Iteration algorithms to work. This is not always possible for real-world problems, we discuss the reasons and possible ways around this in the next subsection.

Other functions that need to be implemented are:

  • step(): perform the given action at current state, and return a tuple:
    • observation: for this case, it is just the next state.
    • reward
    • terminal: bool indicating whether a terminal state is reached.
    • truncated: a bool indicating premature termination, can be used to indicate stoppage before a terminal state is reached. An example would be a limit on the maximum number of steps the agent can take.
    • arbitrary dictionary containing additional info for logging/debugging.
  • reset(): reset the state to a start position, and return a tuple of (observation, info dictionary) as defined above
  • [optional] render(): GUI or string representation of the environment for debugging or visualization

If you’re following the notebook, the agent should be able to reach the resource cell with highest value of (reward – distance from start cell) using policies learnt by all the above algorithms. The notebook uses a 10×10 grid, so it is a fairly simple problem for all algorithms. For rendering, we pretty-print the grid with ‘X’ representing an obstacle, ‘.’ representing an empty cell and ‘R(value)’ representing a resource cell with the corresponding reward value. Also, a boxed ‘[ ]’ cell denotes the agent’s current position.

Transition Probabilities

Obtaining accurate transition probabilities for an MDP can be very challenging in many real-world scenarios. Some reasons that lead to this are:

  • Large state spaces (think a continuous state-space MDP) can make storing the transition matrix impossible
  • Systems are often intricate and involve factors beyond our complete control. It might be impractical or impossible to capture every possible state transition and its probability.
  • Real-world environments often have inherent stochasticity and the outcome might vary due to unforeseen factors.
  • In many cases, the agent might not have access to all the information about its environment, making it challenging to determine the exact state and the resulting transition probabilities.

PI and VI algorithms rely on the exact transition matrix to converge to an optimal policy. Excluding continuous state space states for now (see next section for that), one approach to get around these limitations is to estimate the transition function. This can be done in controlled MDP trials, where the agent is allowed to explore, and we collect samples for (state, action, next_state, reward). These samples can be used to estimate the transition matrix. By incorporating domain knowledge, we can get more samples from stages of an episode with more certainty, in order to increase the confidence in those estimates.

Bridging the Gap: Tackling MDPs with Continuous State Spaces

In our exploration of MDPs so far, we’ve primarily focused on environments with discrete state spaces. However, many real-world scenarios involve continuous state variables, making direct application of standard VI and PI algorithms challenging. For example, consider the CartPole problem [4], where a cart can move along a 1d track and has a pole attached on it through a hinge which constrains the pole’s rotation in the plane along the track (see figure below). The action space consists of {+1, -1} which correspond to applying a fixed force to the cart along the positive or negative x-axis. The goal of the agent is to keep the pole upright for as long as possible, so a reward of +1 is given for every timestep that the MDP continues. Termination happens when the pole falls off (its angle with the vertical exceeds a certain threshold). The state can be described by four continuous variables: cart’s position and velocity, and the pole’s angle with the vertical and angular velocity, making this a continuous state space MDP.

The continuous nature of the state variables leads to an infinitely large number of possible states, rendering exact representation within algorithms impractical. Additionally, as the number of continuous state dimensions increases, the computational complexity of algorithms designed for discrete spaces explodes. This section touches on some approaches for handling MDPs with continuous state spaces.

In a basic grid-based discretization setup, we divide each continuous attributes into intervals, thereby creating a multidimensional grid (four dimensions for the CartPole problem). Each cell in the grid can then be mapped to a discrete state and we could then apply a model-free learning algorithm like Q-learning or estimate the transition probabilities to use PI and VI algorithms. The discretization granularity becomes an important hyperparameter here because the state space size increases as the inverse of the “bin size”. With 4 continuous dimensions, this can already become too large to use PI or VI algorithms we discussed. On the other hand, large bin sizes can end up masking important distinctions needed for decision making. Understanding this trade-off between finer resolution and computational cost motivates the need for careful tuning or looking for alternative approaches.

Often times, the value function for a large continuous state space can be approximated as a linear function of a much smaller feature space. Radial basis function and fourier series analysis are some common approaches on how to extract the non-linear feature space representation of the original state space. We can use this approximate value function to define an approximate Q-value function over the states, action and feature space. Refer to Section 2.1 of [5] for more details on this approach. Similar to a linear approximation, we can aim for better non-linear approximations. Deep Q-Networks (DQNs) [6] use deep neural networks to approximate the value function. DQNs also employ strategies like using a Replay buffer to train the network on memories from various stages of an episode in the same batch. An intuition behind this strategy is that it allows the agent to focus on the important decisions, while not forgetting the learnings from the begining of an episode. This allows the agent to capture complex relationships between states and values but requires careful training and can be computationally expensive.

Note: Function approximation in a complex RL environment is an open problem with plenty of exploring for all of you!

Conclusion

In this article, we delved into the fundamentals of Reinforcement Learning with a focus on Markov Decision Processes and their practical implementation using OpenAI Gym. Starting with classic algorithms like Value Iteration, Policy Iteration and Q-Learinng on a simple MDP, we explored the complexities of designing custom MDPs and the challenges posed by continuous state spaces. Reinforcement Learning provides a powerful framework for modeling sequential decision-making problems in diverse domains. While the core concepts are rooted in dynamic programming, their successful application in real-world scenarios demands adaptability. Whether it’s designing custom environments tailored to your problem, obtaining transition probabilities, or tackling the complexities of continuous states, understanding the limitations and potential solutions is key.

References
